There's a word that people dread. It's know. But in fact, getting a know can create a moment of power for you as I outlined in the previous clip. Now, one way to turn that know into a yes is to offer a concession. But it's important to be clear on a common mistake that many people make, to think that straight discounts are good concessions. You might think, if someone says no to my product or service, I might get them to say yes by just providing a price discount. However, when you offer a discount to someone who has already said no to your proposal, you stand to lose credibility in the eyes of that person, who might well think that you were overpricing your product or service in the first place and trying to take advantage of them. What's more, your prospect might wonder if your price could be even lower. If you gave them a five percent discount, they might push you to give them even more because your first price clearly wasn't the lowest you could offer. Now, don't get me wrong, discounts can work, but I'll add this caveat. If you pitch one price and then agree to cut it, you have to have a meaningful reason for the discount, which could be that the lower price would be given as part of a volume buy or a package deal or a commitment to future purchases or even a reduction of the features that you could provide at that discounted price. Without those sensible reasons, you'll lose trust-worthy.
